title: Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis in a 75-Year-Old with AF
subtitle: Type 2 (Destructive) AIT — Why Methimazole Failed and the Steroid Pivot
authors: Clinical case vignette — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Follow-up of thyrotoxicosis with persistently high Free T4 despite maximum-dose methimazole

caseDescription:
A 75-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and recent-onset thyrotoxicosis presents for follow-up after one month of maximum-dose methimazole (60 mg/day). His AF is well controlled at 80 bpm on bisoprolol. Despite the aggressive antithyroid regimen, laboratory results show a persistently surging Free T4 while T3 remains normal. Hepatic transaminases and absolute neutrophil count (ANC) are stable.

Because of the lack of clinical response, a color-flow Doppler ultrasound of the thyroid is performed, revealing a normal-appearing gland with a lack of hypervascularity (no ‘inferno’ pattern). The picture is that of Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis (AIT), predominantly Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis) or mixed AIT.

Management is pivoted to the destructive, inflammatory nature of the condition: bisoprolol is maintained for rate control; methimazole is de-escalated to a holding dose of 20–30 mg/day to reduce toxicity while covering possible mixed pathology; oral prednisolone 30–40 mg/day is started to halt follicular leakage; and cholestyramine 4 g two-to-three times daily (staggered) is added as an intraluminal sink to accelerate clearance of circulating T4.
keyFindings: Persistently surging Free T4 with normal T3 despite 1 month of methimazole 60 mg/day
AF well controlled (HR 80 bpm) on bisoprolol
Thyroid color-flow Doppler: normal gland, NO hypervascularity (no ‘inferno’ pattern) → destructive pattern
finalDiagnosis: Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis (AIT), predominantly Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis) or mixed AIT
learningObjectives: Distinguish Type 1 (iodine-induced synthesis) from Type 2 (destructive) AIT using Doppler vascularity and the T4/T3 pattern
Explain why methimazole is mechanistically ineffective in pure Type 2 AIT and pivot to glucocorticoids
Apply adjuncts (cholestyramine, beta-blocker choice) and dose/route rationale appropriate to a 75-year-old

items.title: How the treatment was pivoted from anti-synthesis to anti-inflammatory
items.content: Once a destructive (Type 2 / mixed) process was recognised, the strategy shifted from blocking synthesis to halting leakage and clearing circulating hormone. Bisoprolol is maintained for rate control. Methimazole is de-escalated to a holding dose of 20–30 mg/day — low enough to cut the dose-dependent toxicity risk, high enough to cover any coexisting iodine-driven synthesis (mixed AIT). Oral prednisolone 30–40 mg/day is started to stabilise follicular membranes and stop the inflammatory leak. Cholestyramine 4 g two-to-three times daily (staggered from other drugs) is added to bind hormone in the gut and accelerate its excretion.
keyPoints: Methimazole reduced to 20–30 mg/day holding dose (toxicity vs mixed-pathology cover).
Prednisolone 30–40 mg/day halts follicular leakage; cholestyramine speeds T4 clearance.

items.question: Why is propranolol the drug of choice in the acute phase of Graves’ thyrotoxicosis?
items.answer: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that, at higher doses (≥160 mg/day), uniquely inhibits the 5'-deiodinase enzyme. This prevents the peripheral conversion of T4 to the more active T3, giving a direct anti-thyroid physiological benefit on top of heart-rate control.

items.question: What is the main drawback of using propranolol for rate control?
items.answer: It has a short half-life, so it requires frequent dosing (3–4 times daily) unless an extended-release formulation is used.

items.question: Why is bisoprolol preferred for long-term maintenance in this patient?
items.answer: Bisoprolol is a highly cardioselective β1 antagonist that gives stable, 24-hour rate control with convenient once-daily dosing, and carries a much lower risk of bronchospasm or adverse metabolic effects than non-selective agents.

items.question: Why should atenolol be avoided or used with caution compared to bisoprolol?
items.answer: Atenolol is hydrophilic with a shorter effective half-life, often requiring twice-daily dosing, and it relies heavily on renal clearance — a concern in an elderly patient.

items.question: In what specific scenario is esmolol indicated?
items.answer: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting intravenous β1 blocker reserved strictly for acute inpatient or intensive-care settings — an unstable thyroid storm or a rapid ventricular rate causing haemodynamic compromise.

items.question: Why does switching this 75-year-old to propranolol offer no additional clinical benefit?
items.answer: His T3 is already normal: the T4-to-T3 conversion pathway is already blunted (by amiodarone), so high-dose propranolol offers no metabolic upside. His heart rate is also already well controlled on bisoprolol.

items.question: What specific risks does propranolol pose for a 75-year-old patient?
items.answer: Being highly lipophilic, it readily crosses the blood–brain barrier, raising the risk of CNS effects such as confusion, lethargy, and sleep disturbance. Its β2 blockade can also trigger bronchospasm or mask recovery from hypoglycaemia.

items.question: Why should the methimazole dose not be increased beyond 60 mg/day?
items.answer: At 60 mg/day, thyroid peroxidase (TPO) is already maximally inhibited. Increasing the dose does not suppress new hormone synthesis any further or faster, but it exponentially increases the risk of severe, dose-dependent toxicities.

items.question: What are the life-threatening toxicities of pushing methimazole beyond 60 mg/day?
items.answer: The primary risks are agranulocytosis and cholestatic hepatotoxicity.

items.question: Why does the patient still have high Free T4 after a month on maximum-dose methimazole?
items.answer: The thyroid holds a large reservoir of pre-formed hormone in the colloid. Methimazole only halts new synthesis; it typically takes 4–8 weeks for the pre-existing stores to deplete and clear from the bloodstream.

items.question: How does cholestyramine accelerate clearance of thyroid hormone in thyrotoxicosis?
items.answer: Up to 15–20% of circulating T4 and T3 undergo enterohepatic recirculation. Cholestyramine acts as an intraluminal sink, binding these hormones in the gut and forcing their excretion in faeces, which accelerates clearance of the high Free T4 pool.

items.question: How must cholestyramine be administered to prevent drug interactions?
items.answer: It must be staggered — taken at least 1 hour after or 4 hours before drugs such as bisoprolol and methimazole — so the resin does not bind them in the gut and nullify their absorption.

items.question: How does amiodarone explain the laboratory profile of high Free T4 and normal T3?
items.answer: Amiodarone structurally resembles thyroxine, carries a massive iodine load, and strongly inhibits the 5'-deiodinase enzyme. This blocks the peripheral conversion of T4 to T3, so T4 surges while T3 stays normal.

items.question: What is Type 1 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT)?
items.answer: It is iodine-induced hyperthyroidism: the massive iodine load from amiodarone fuels over-synthesis of thyroid hormone in a gland with underlying autonomous pathology (such as latent Graves’ disease or a multinodular goiter).

items.question: What is Type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT)?
items.answer: It is a destructive thyroiditis: amiodarone exerts a direct cytotoxic effect on the thyroid follicles, causing them to rupture and release pre-formed colloid hormone into the bloodstream.

items.question: Why is methimazole mechanistically useless against pure Type 2 AIT?
items.answer: Methimazole works by blocking the synthesis of new thyroid hormone. In Type 2 AIT there is no new synthesis occurring — the thyrotoxicosis is a pure leakage phenomenon from glandular destruction, so there is nothing for methimazole to block.

items.question: How does a color-flow Doppler ultrasound differentiate between the two types of AIT?
items.answer: High vascularity (an ‘inferno’ pattern) points to Type 1 (active synthesis), whereas low or absent vascularity points to Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis).

items.question: What makes glucocorticoids (prednisolone) the optimal treatment for Type 2 AIT?
items.answer: Steroids rapidly stabilise follicular membranes and halt the destructive inflammatory process. They also give a secondary benefit by further inhibiting peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion.

items.question: Why is the methimazole dose reduced to 20–30 mg/day instead of being stopped completely once Type 2 AIT is suspected?
items.answer: A moderate holding dose covers the common possibility of mixed AIT (some iodine-driven synthesis occurring alongside the destruction), while simultaneously reducing the high risk of hepatotoxicity and agranulocytosis associated with the 60 mg dose.

items.question: Why does amiodarone continue to drive thyroid pathology long after the drug is discontinued?
items.answer: Amiodarone is highly lipophilic with a massive volume of distribution and a terminal half-life of 50–100 days, so the drug and its iodine load remain in the patient’s system for months.

items.stem: Doppler shows a non-hypervascular gland and the picture is destructive (Type 2) AIT. Which is the most appropriate primary pharmacological treatment?
options: Increase methimazole to 80 mg/day
Oral glucocorticoids (e.g. prednisolone 30–40 mg/day)
High-dose iodine (Lugol’s solution)
Radioactive iodine ablation
items.explanation: Type 2 AIT is a destructive thyroiditis, so the primary treatment is glucocorticoids, which stabilise follicular membranes, halt the inflammatory leak, and secondarily reduce T4-to-T3 conversion. Raising methimazole only adds toxicity (no synthesis to block), iodine would worsen an iodine-loaded gland, and radioiodine is ineffective when uptake is suppressed.

items.stem: Why does this amiodarone-treated patient show a surging Free T4 but a normal T3?
options: Amiodarone increases TSH secretion
Amiodarone inhibits 5'-deiodinase, blocking peripheral conversion of T4 to T3
Methimazole selectively raises T4
The thyroid is producing only T4 and no T3
items.explanation: Amiodarone strongly inhibits 5'-deiodinase, the enzyme that converts T4 to the more active T3 peripherally. With conversion blocked, T4 accumulates and rises while T3 stays normal — the classic biochemical signature.

items.stem: Which beta-blocker is the ultra-short-acting IV agent reserved for unstable thyroid storm or haemodynamic compromise?
options: Atenolol
Bisoprolol
Esmolol
Propranolol
items.explanation: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting intravenous β1 blocker used in acute inpatient/ICU settings for unstable thyroid storm or a rapid ventricular rate causing haemodynamic compromise; the others are oral agents for ongoing rate control.

items.statement: In pure Type 2 AIT, methimazole is ineffective because there is no new hormone synthesis for it to block.
items.explanation: True. Type 2 AIT is a destructive leak of pre-formed hormone, not active synthesis, so a synthesis blocker like methimazole has nothing to act on.

items.statement: Because amiodarone is cleared quickly, its thyroid effects resolve within a few days of stopping the drug.
items.explanation: False. Amiodarone is highly lipophilic with a huge volume of distribution and a terminal half-life of 50–100 days, so the drug and its iodine load — and the thyroid effects — persist for months.

items.assertion: Methimazole alone is inadequate for pure Type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis.
items.reason: Type 2 AIT is a destructive release of pre-formed hormone with no ongoing synthesis for methimazole to block.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Because the thyrotoxicosis in Type 2 AIT comes from follicular destruction rather than synthesis, a synthesis blocker cannot control it — which is exactly why glucocorticoids, not methimazole, are the mainstay.

items.stem: Why is the patient’s Free T4 still high after a month on maximum-dose methimazole?
options: The gland’s large pre-formed colloid reservoir takes about 4–8 weeks to deplete and clear
Methimazole paradoxically increases T4 synthesis
The 60 mg dose is too low to have any effect
It must be a laboratory error
items.explanation: Methimazole only halts new synthesis; it does nothing to the large reservoir of pre-formed hormone stored in the colloid, which typically takes 4–8 weeks to deplete and clear. So a persistently high Free T4 at one month is expected, not a sign of treatment failure or lab error.

items.stem: Why is increasing methimazole above 60 mg/day not helpful in this patient?
options: Thyroid peroxidase is already maximally inhibited, so only the toxicity risk rises
Higher doses block hormone release from the colloid
Higher doses accelerate clearance of stored hormone
Higher doses improve peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion
items.explanation: At 60 mg/day, thyroid peroxidase (TPO) is already maximally inhibited, so pushing the dose higher cannot suppress synthesis any further — it only multiplies the dose-dependent risks of agranulocytosis and hepatotoxicity. Methimazole does not act on hormone release, stored-hormone clearance, or peripheral conversion.

items.stem: Which two toxicities are the main dose-dependent dangers of high-dose methimazole?
options: Agranulocytosis and cholestatic hepatotoxicity
Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity
Pulmonary fibrosis and peripheral neuropathy
QT prolongation and rhabdomyolysis
items.explanation: The two life-threatening, dose-dependent toxicities of methimazole are agranulocytosis and cholestatic hepatotoxicity — the reason ANC and transaminases are monitored and the dose is minimised once it offers no extra synthetic blockade.

items.stem: By what mechanism does cholestyramine lower circulating thyroid hormone?
options: It binds T4 and T3 in the gut, interrupting enterohepatic recirculation and increasing faecal excretion
It blocks thyroid peroxidase
It inhibits 5'-deiodinase
It suppresses pituitary TSH release
items.explanation: Up to 15–20% of circulating T4/T3 undergo enterohepatic recirculation; cholestyramine binds them in the gut lumen and forces faecal excretion, acting as an intraluminal sink that accelerates clearance of the high Free T4 pool. It does not affect TPO, deiodinase, or TSH.

items.stem: A thyroid color-flow Doppler showing a hypervascular ‘inferno’ pattern most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Type 1 AIT (iodine-driven active synthesis)
Type 2 AIT (destructive thyroiditis)
A normal gland
Central (pituitary) hyperthyroidism
items.explanation: High vascularity (the ‘inferno’ pattern) reflects an actively synthesising, hyperaemic gland and points to Type 1 AIT. Type 2 (destructive) AIT shows low or absent vascularity, as in this patient.

items.stem: Which beta-blocker best provides convenient once-daily, cardioselective long-term rate control in this 75-year-old?
options: Bisoprolol
Propranolol
Esmolol
Atenolol
items.explanation: Bisoprolol is a highly cardioselective β1 antagonist giving stable 24-hour control with once-daily dosing and a low risk of bronchospasm or CNS effects — ideal for an elderly patient. Propranolol is non-selective, lipophilic, and short-acting; esmolol is IV-only; atenolol is renally cleared and often twice-daily.

items.statement: Bisoprolol is a cardioselective β1 blocker suitable for convenient once-daily long-term rate control.
items.explanation: True. Bisoprolol’s cardioselectivity and long duration make it well suited to stable, once-daily maintenance rate control, with a lower risk of bronchospasm and metabolic effects than non-selective agents.

items.statement: High-dose propranolol (≥160 mg/day) inhibits 5'-deiodinase and reduces peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.
items.explanation: True. At higher doses propranolol uniquely inhibits 5'-deiodinase, blunting T4-to-T3 conversion — a benefit in acute Graves’ thyrotoxicosis, though redundant here where T3 is already normal.

items.statement: A hypervascular ‘inferno’ pattern on color-flow Doppler indicates Type 2 destructive AIT.
items.explanation: False. The hypervascular ‘inferno’ pattern indicates Type 1 AIT (active synthesis). Type 2 destructive thyroiditis shows low or absent vascularity.

items.statement: Cholestyramine can be taken at the same time as methimazole and bisoprolol without affecting their absorption.
items.explanation: False. Cholestyramine is a binding resin that will bind co-administered drugs in the gut; it must be staggered (at least 1 hour after or 4 hours before methimazole and bisoprolol) to avoid nullifying their absorption.

items.statement: Glucocorticoids used for Type 2 AIT also further inhibit peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.
items.explanation: True. Beyond stabilising follicular membranes and halting the destructive inflammation, glucocorticoids give a secondary benefit by further inhibiting peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion.

items.stem: What is the main drawback of using propranolol for rate control?
options: A: It has a short half-life, so it requires frequent dosing (3–4 times daily) unless an extended-release formulation is used.
B: It causes significant peripheral vasoconstriction and hypotension.
C: It increases the risk of bronchoconstriction in patients with asthma.
D: It is associated with a high incidence of heart failure exacerbation.
items.explanation: Propranolol's short half-life necessitates frequent dosing unless an extended-release formulation is used, which can impact patient adherence.

items.stem: Why is bisoprolol preferred for long-term maintenance in a patient requiring β-blocker therapy?
options: A) It has a longer half-life than other β-blockers, reducing the frequency of dosing
B) It is a highly cardioselective β1 antagonist with 24-hour rate control and once-daily dosing, with lower risk of bronchospasm and metabolic adverse effects
C) It is associated with greater reduction in blood pressure compared to other β-blockers
D) It is less likely to cause peripheral vasoconstriction than non-selective β-blockers
items.explanation: Bisoprolol's cardioselectivity, 24-hour efficacy, once-daily dosing, and reduced risk of bronchospasm and metabolic effects make it optimal for long-term use.

items.stem: Esmolol is indicated in which of the following scenarios?
options: A. Unstable thyroid storm or rapid ventricular rate causing hemodynamic compromise in acute inpatient settings
B. Propranolol-resistant migraine prophylaxis
C. Chronic hypertension management in outpatient settings
D. Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
items.explanation: Esmolol's ultra-short half-life and intravenous route make it ideal for acute hemodynamic instability (e.g., thyroid storm, rapid ventricular rate), unlike other beta-blockers used for chronic conditions or different indications.

items.assertion: Switching this patient to propranolol offers no additional clinical benefit.
items.reason: Amiodarone has already blunted T4-to-T3 conversion, rendering high-dose propranolol metabolically ineffective, and bisoprolol adequately controls heart rate.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Amiodarone's inhibition of T4-to-T3 conversion and bisoprolol's effective heart rate control eliminate potential benefits of propranolol in this context.

items.stem: Why should the methimazole dose not be increased beyond 60 mg/day?
options: A) At 60 mg/day, thyroid peroxidase (TPO) is already maximally inhibited. Increasing the dose does not suppress new hormone synthesis any further or faster, but it exponentially increases the risk of severe, dose-dependent toxicities.
B) Methimazole becomes immediately ineffective beyond 60 mg/day due to rapid metabolic degradation.
C) The 60 mg/day dose is the maximum approved by regulatory agencies for long-term use.
D) Increasing the dose beyond 60 mg/day causes rapid development of thyroid hormone resistance in patients.
items.explanation: At 60 mg/day, TPO inhibition is maximal, and higher doses do not enhance suppression but increase toxicity risks.

items.stem: Why does a patient with hyperthyroidism still have elevated free T4 levels after 1 month of maximum-dose methimazole therapy?
options: A) Methimazole inhibits TSH secretion, delaying feedback regulation
B) The thyroid gland rapidly converts T4 to T3, bypassing methimazole's effects
C) Pre-formed thyroid hormone stored in follicular colloid persists for 4–8 weeks before clearing
D) Methimazole induces hepatic enzyme activity, accelerating T4 metabolism
items.explanation: Methimazole blocks thyroid hormone synthesis but does not affect existing hormone stores in the thyroid follicles. These pre-formed hormones remain in the colloid and are gradually released into circulation over 4–8 weeks.

items.stem: How does cholestyramine accelerate clearance of thyroid hormone in thyrotoxicosis?
options: A) By inhibiting thyroid hormone synthesis in the thyroid gland
B) By enhancing renal excretion of T4 and T3
C) By acting as an intraluminal sink, binding T4 and T3 in the gut and promoting fecal excretion
D) By blocking thyroid hormone receptors in target tissues
items.explanation: Cholestyramine binds thyroid hormones in the gastrointestinal tract, interrupting enterohepatic recirculation and increasing fecal excretion of T4 and T3.

items.stem: How should cholestyramine be administered to avoid interactions with drugs like bisoprolol and methimazole?
options: A) Staggered administration — at least 1 hour after or 4 hours before drug ingestion
B) Concurrent administration with food to enhance absorption
C) 2 hours before drug intake to reduce gut binding
D) Simultaneous administration with the drug to ensure complete resin binding
items.explanation: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant that binds drugs in the gastrointestinal tract. To prevent absorption inhibition, it must be administered at least 1 hour after or 4 hours before other medications.

items.stem: Which mechanism best explains the laboratory finding of elevated free T4 and normal T3 levels in a patient taking amiodarone?
options: A) Structural similarity to thyroxine, high iodine content, and inhibition of 5'-deiodinase enzyme
B) Increased hepatic clearance of T3 with unchanged T4 metabolism
C) Iodine deficiency-induced thyrotropin (TSH) stimulation of the thyroid
D) Direct stimulation of thyroid hormone synthesis by amiodarone
items.explanation: Amiodarone inhibits 5'-deiodinase, blocking peripheral conversion of T4 to T3, leading to elevated free T4 and preserved T3 levels.

items.stem: What is Type 1 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT)?
options: A) Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism due to excessive iodine from amiodarone in a gland with autonomous pathology (e.g., multinodular goiter)
B) Autoimmune destruction of thyroid tissue leading to hormone release
C) Iodine deficiency-induced hypothyroidism
D) Inhibition of thyroid hormone synthesis by amiodarone
items.explanation: Type 1 AIT occurs when amiodarone's high iodine content stimulates hormone production in a thyroid with preexisting autonomous function (e.g., multinodular goiter), unlike Type 2 AIT which involves thyroiditis.

items.stem: Why is methimazole ineffective in treating pure Type 2 autoimmune thyroiditis (AIT)?
options: A) It increases thyroid hormone uptake by peripheral tissues
B) It stimulates thyroid gland destruction
C) It has no effect on thyroid hormone synthesis
D) It is metabolized by the same enzymes as thyroid hormones
items.explanation: Methimazole inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis, but in Type 2 AIT, thyrotoxicosis results from preformed hormone leakage due to gland destruction, not ongoing synthesis.

items.stem: Which finding on color-flow Doppler ultrasound best differentiates Type 1 from Type 2 autoimmune thyroiditis (AIT)?
options: A) High vascularity (‘inferno’ pattern) in Type 1 vs low/absent vascularity in Type 2
B) Low vascularity in Type 1 vs high vascularity in Type 2
C) Echogenicity changes in Type 1 vs architectural distortion in Type 2
D) Thyroid size enlargement in Type 1 vs normal size in Type 2
items.explanation: Type 1 AIT (active synthesis) shows high vascularity on Doppler, while Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis) shows low or absent vascularity due to tissue destruction.

items.stem: What is the primary mechanism by which glucocorticoids (e.g., prednisolone) are the optimal treatment for Type 2 autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis)?
options: A) Rapid inhibition of thyroid peroxidase activity
B) Stabilization of follicular membranes and suppression of T4-to-T3 peripheral conversion
C) Induction of thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibodies
D) Enhancement of thyroid hormone uptake by target tissues
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids stabilize thyroid follicular membranes to halt inflammation and inhibit peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion, reducing thyroid hormone levels and inflammation in Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

items.stem: Why is the methimazole dose reduced to 20–30 mg/day instead of being stopped completely once Type 2 AIT is suspected?
options: A) A moderate holding dose covers the common possibility of mixed AIT (some iodine-driven synthesis occurring alongside the destruction), while simultaneously reducing the high risk of hepatotoxicity and agranulocytosis associated with the 60 mg dose.
B) To prevent hypothyroidism caused by complete drug withdrawal.
C) To minimize long-term costs of treatment.
D) To avoid thyroid storm associated with abrupt dose changes.
items.explanation: A moderate holding dose balances the risk of ongoing thyroid dysfunction with the safety profile of lower methimazole doses, as higher doses carry significant risks of hepatotoxicity and agranulocytosis.

items.stem: Why does amiodarone continue to drive thyroid pathology long after the drug is discontinued?
options: A) Amiodarone contains high levels of iodine, which persists in the thyroid gland after discontinuation
B) Amiodarone directly inhibits thyroid hormone receptors, causing prolonged effects
C) Amiodarone is highly lipophilic with a massive volume of distribution and a terminal half-life of 50–100 days, so the drug and its iodine load remain in the patient’s system for months
D) Amiodarone induces autoimmune thyroiditis that is independent of drug levels
items.explanation: Amiodarone's lipophilicity, large volume of distribution, and prolonged terminal half-life (50–100 days) result in persistent drug and iodine exposure, explaining delayed thyroid effects after discontinuation.

items.stem: Why is propranolol preferred over other beta-blockers in the acute management of Graves’ thyrotoxicosis?
options: A) It selectively blocks beta-1 receptors to reduce cardiac symptoms
B) It inhibits 5'-deiodinase, reducing peripheral conversion of T4 to T3
C) It has intrinsic sympathomimetic activity to counteract thyrotoxicosis
D) It increases thyroid hormone binding to plasma proteins
items.explanation: Propranolol's non-selective beta-blockade and inhibition of 5'-deiodinase enzyme uniquely reduce T3 levels, providing additional anti-thyroid effects beyond cardiovascular control.

items.stem: What are the life-threatening toxicities of administering methimazole beyond 60 mg/day?
options: A. Agranulocytosis and cholestatic hepatotoxicity
B. Hypothyroidism and cutaneous rash
C. Acute liver failure and nephrotoxicity
D. Neutropenia and gastrointestinal bleeding
items.explanation: Methimazole doses >60 mg/day increase risks of agranulocytosis (severe neutropenia) and cholestatic hepatotoxicity, which are life-threatening.

items.stem: What is the mechanism of Type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT)?
options: A) Destructive thyroiditis due to direct cytotoxic effects of amiodarone on thyroid follicles
B) Autoimmune thyroiditis with TSH receptor antibodies
C) Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism from excess iodine intake
D) Thyroid adenoma with autonomous hormone secretion
items.explanation: Type 2 AIT results from amiodarone's direct cytotoxic effect on thyroid follicles, causing rupture and release of pre-formed thyroid hormone.

items.stem: Why should atenolol be avoided or used with caution compared to bisoprolol?
options: A. Atenolol is hydrophilic with a shorter effective half-life, often requiring twice-daily dosing, and it relies heavily on renal clearance — a concern in an elderly patient.
B. Atenolol has greater β1-selectivity than bisoprolol, making it more suitable for patients with asthma.
C. Atenolol is metabolized primarily by the liver, increasing the risk of drug interactions.
D. Atenolol has a longer duration of action, necessitating once-daily dosing but causing greater risk of bradycardia.
items.explanation: Atenolol's hydrophilicity, shorter half-life, and renal dependence (vs. bisoprolol's lipophilicity, longer half-life, and hepatic metabolism) make it less favorable in renal impairment or elderly patients.

items.stem: What specific risks does propranolol pose for a 75-year-old patient?
options: A. Being highly lipophilic, it readily crosses the blood–brain barrier, raising the risk of CNS effects such as confusion, lethargy, and sleep disturbance. Its β2 blockade can also trigger bronchospasm or mask recovery from hypoglycaemia.
B. It causes peripheral vasoconstriction and edema due to α1-adrenergic blockade, increasing the risk of heart failure in elderly patients.
C. It may precipitate heart block in patients with preexisting conduction system disease due to its nonselective β-blockade.
D. It increases the risk of arrhythmias by prolonging the QT interval, particularly in patients with electrolyte imbalances.
items.explanation: Propranolol's lipophilicity allows it to cross the blood–brain barrier, increasing CNS side effects in elderly patients. Its β2 blockade can exacerbate bronchospasm and mask hypoglycemia symptoms, which are critical considerations in older adults.
